chapter_metadata.extraction_notes:
Extracted the first 7 solved examples from the textbook,
separating multi-part questions into individual, clean, distinct templates.

formulas.formula_latex:
ext{Profit \%} = \left(\dfrac{	ext{S.P.} -
	ext{C.P.}}{	ext{C.P.}}ight) 	imes 100
formulas.formula_description:
Calculates
the percentage gain over the original cost price. Used when Selling Price
exceeds Cost Price.

formulas.formula_latex:
ext{Loss \%} = \left(\dfrac{	ext{C.P.} -
	ext{S.P.}}{	ext{C.P.}}ight) 	imes 100
formulas.formula_description:
Calculates
the percentage loss incurred relative to the cost price. Used when Cost Price
exceeds Selling Price.

Finds the final sale price given the
cost price and a desired profit percentage.

Finds
the initial cost price given the final sale price and the profit percentage
achieved.

integer_mode.description:
Parameters chosen so that the profit
percentage is an integer value.

constraints.reason:
Calculated
percentage profit must divide cleanly into an integer

decimal_mode.description:
Parameters chosen so that the profit
percentage is a non-integer decimal.

constraints.reason:
Calculated
percentage profit results in a decimal value

fraction_mode.description:
Parameters chosen so that the profit
percentage is a decimal, but constrained for simpler fraction visual outputs.

constraints.reason: Ensure a clean fractional representation

templates.question_template:
{{agent_1_name}} purchased a
{{item_name}} for Rs. {{cost_price}} and later sold it for Rs.
{{selling_price}}. What is the percent profit she made on the {{item_name}}?

steps.explanation:
First, calculate the absolute
profit earned by subtracting the cost price (C.P.) from the selling price
(S.P.).

Next,
compute the profit percentage relative to the cost price by dividing the profit
by the cost price and multiplying by 100.

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
Express the relationship of Selling Price to Cost Price directly
to bypass step-by-step subtraction.
shortcut_solution.when_to_use:
Best applied when numbers
can be simplified cleanly to small ratios.

steps.explanation:
Express the profit percentage directly using the unified ratio
formula.

distractors.mistake_description:
Calculated the profit percentage on the Selling Price instead of the Cost
Price.
Inverted the terms, dividing the Cost Price by the absolute profit.

A minor
arithmetic slip in the percentage conversion step.

integer_mode.description:
Cost Price and
Selling Price chosen so that the loss percentage is an integer value.

constraints.reason: Loss percentage must be a whole number

decimal_mode.description:
Cost Price and
Selling Price chosen so that the loss percentage is a decimal.

constraints.reason: Loss percentage results in a non-integer decimal value

fraction_mode.description:
Values configured
for rational visual division with clean remainders.

constraints.reason: Ensures clean fractional results

templates.question_template:
If the cost price is Rs. {{cost_price}} and the selling
price is Rs. {{selling_price}}, find the percentage loss.

steps.explanation:
First, calculate the absolute loss incurred by subtracting the
selling price (S.P.) from the cost price (C.P.).

Compute the loss percentage relative to the
cost price by dividing the loss by the cost price and multiplying by 100.
steps.latex:
ext{Loss \%} = \left(\dfrac{	ext{Loss}}{	ext{C.P.}}ight)
	imes 100 = \left(\dfrac{ {{loss_amount}} }{ {{cost_price}} }ight) 	imes 100
= {{loss_percent}}\%

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
Express loss percentage directly as a simplified ratio of
Selling Price to Cost Price subtracted from unity.
shortcut_solution.when_to_use:
Helpful to
mentally approximate loss without intermediate subtraction steps.

steps.explanation:
Express loss directly based on the difference
of S.P. from the unity cost factor.

distractors.mistake_description:
Erroneously
computed the loss percentage on the basis of Selling Price.

Calculated the percentage recovery instead of
the percentage loss.
Minor calculation error in final division.

integer_mode.description:
Values are aligned so that the resulting
Selling Price is an exact integer.

constraints.reason:
Calculated
Selling Price has no fractional remainder

decimal_mode.description:
Values are configured to allow
non-integer/cents decimal outputs.

constraints.reason:
Selling price
contains a cents portion

fraction_mode.description: Parameters selected for easy fractional representation.

constraints.reason: Produces fractional cents values

templates.question_template:
{{agent_1_name}} purchased a {{item_name}} for Rs.
{{cost_price}}. If he sells it at a gain of {{profit_percent}}%, find the
selling price.

steps.explanation:
Since there is a gain of {{profit_percent}}%,
the Selling Price must equal {{profit_factor}}% of the Cost Price.

Calculate the value by
multiplying the Cost Price by the percentage factor over 100.

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
A gain of P% is
equivalent to scaling the initial cost by a decimal multiplier of (1 + P/100).
shortcut_solution.when_to_use:
Extremely fast when performing quick mental math or writing
direct equations.

steps.explanation:
Multiply the
Cost Price directly by the decimal scaling factor.

distractors.mistake_description:
Subtracted the percentage
instead of adding it, as if calculating a loss.

Added the percentage value directly as a flat monetary
value instead of a rate.

Used the division-based reverse lookup formula
incorrectly.

integer_mode.description:
Values are aligned so that the resulting
Selling Price is an exact integer.

constraints.reason:
Calculated Selling
Price has no fractional remainder under loss

decimal_mode.description:
Values are configured to allow
non-integer/cents decimal outputs.

constraints.reason:
Selling price
contains a cents portion under loss

fraction_mode.description:
Parameters selected for clean fractional
remainders.

constraints.reason: Produces fractional cents values

templates.question_template:
{{agent_1_name}} bought a
{{item_name}} for Rs. {{cost_price}}. If she sells it at a loss of
{{loss_percent}}%, find the selling price.

steps.explanation:
Since there is a loss of
{{loss_percent}}%, the Selling Price must equal {{loss_factor}}% of the Cost
Price.

Calculate the final
price by multiplying the Cost Price by the discounted percentage factor
over 100.

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
A loss of L%
equates to multiplying the original cost by (1 - L/100).
shortcut_solution.when_to_use:
Ideal
for fast calculations when multiplying small numbers.

steps.explanation:
Multiply the Cost Price directly by the
relative decimal scale factor.

distractors.mistake_description:
Added the percentage instead of subtracting it,
as if calculating a gain.
Subtracted the percentage value directly as a flat currency amount.

Incorrectly applied a
division-based markup multiplier instead of subtraction.

classification.pattern_variant:
Cost Price
from Loss and Selling Price

integer_mode.description: Output cost price yields a clean, non-fractional integer.

constraints.reason: Cost Price must divide evenly into a whole number

decimal_mode.description:
Calculated cost price yields a fractional
decimal value.

constraints.reason:
Calculated Cost Price has fractional decimal
units
Cost Price is fractional

templates.question_template:
A {{item_name}} is sold for Rs. {{selling_price}} at a
loss of {{loss_percent}}%. What is the cost price of the {{item_name}}?

steps.explanation:
Since there is a loss of {{loss_percent}}%, the Selling Price of
Rs. {{selling_price}} represents {{net_percentage}}% of the Cost Price.

To determine the full 100% Cost Price, divide the Selling Price by the net
percentage fraction.

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
The cost price is obtained directly by dividing the S.P. by the
multiplier associated with the loss.
shortcut_solution.when_to_use:
Useful for immediate
single-step calculation setups.

steps.explanation: Set up and solve the single-line multiplier reciprocal equation.

distractors.mistake_description:
Added the loss percentage directly to S.P. in a forward
manner instead of reversing the cost fraction.

Inverted the formula by treating the loss percentage as
an additive gain percentage.

Subtracted the loss percentage directly from the selling
price.

classification.pattern_variant:
Cost Price from Markup and Selling
Price

integer_mode.description:
Values are arranged to produce a clean Cost
Price integer.

constraints.reason: Calculated Cost Price has no remainder

decimal_mode.description:
Values
configured to allow fractional decimal outputs for Cost Price.

constraints.reason: Resulting Cost Price is decimal

fraction_mode.description:
Configured for clean division in fractional
formatting.

constraints.reason: Produces fractional costs

templates.question_template:
The owner of a {{item_name}} shop
charges his customer {{markup_percent}}% more than the cost price. If a customer
paid Rs. {{selling_price}} for a {{item_name}}, then what was the cost price of
the {{item_name}}?

steps.explanation:
Since the owner charges {{markup_percent}}%
more than the cost price, the customer's payment represents {{markup_sum}}% of
the original cost price.

Solve for the Cost Price (C.P.) by converting the percentage
value to its fractional equivalent and dividing.

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
A value marked up by M% can be
reverted to original cost by dividing directly by (1 + M/100).
shortcut_solution.when_to_use: Saves writing intermediate proportions when reverse-engineering costs.

steps.explanation:
Divide Selling Price directly by
the decimal markup factor.

distractors.mistake_description:
Incorrectly subtracted
markup from Selling Price directly as if it were a discount.

Directly subtracted the percentage value as a raw
currency unit.

Applied
the markup multiplier on the Selling Price instead of reverse-engineering from
Cost Price.

integer_mode.description:
Configured so that both Cost Price and the Target Selling Price are exact whole
numbers.

constraints.reason: Ensure intermediate Cost Price is an integer

Ensure final adjusted Selling
Price is a clean integer

decimal_mode.description:
Configured to allow decimal outputs for intermediate or final
steps.

constraints.reason: Intermediate steps can contain decimals

fraction_mode.description:
Values set up for
clean division in fractional ratios.

constraints.reason:
Allows
fractional solutions

templates.question_template:
{{agent_1_name}} sold a {{item_name}} at the cost
of Rs. {{selling_price}} at a loss of {{loss_percent}}%. At what cost will she
have to sell it to get a profit of {{target_profit_percent}}%?

steps.explanation:
First, find the Cost Price (C.P.) of the {{item_name}} using the loss
percentage from the first transaction.

Next, compute the required Selling Price to achieve the target profit of
{{target_profit_percent}}% on the C.P.

shortcut_solution.key_insight:
Combine the purchase reversal and markup
adjustment into a single step: New SP = Old SP * (100 + Profit%) / (100 -
Loss%).
shortcut_solution.when_to_use:
Extremely powerful for exams to skip calculating the
intermediate Cost Price entirely.

steps.explanation:
Use the proportional ratio formula directly to find the target
Selling Price.

distractors.mistake_description:
Incorrectly combined percentages additively, applying a flat difference
directly to Selling Price.

Inverted the first
multiplier, treating the initial sale as a markup instead of reverse-engineering
C.P.

Calculated with a final subtraction
error on the selling price calculation.
